result:
The first time I realized that the wallpaper in my parents’ house was alive, I was seven and running a fever of 103. The yellow calico roses puckered like lips and whispered inventory: the number of times I’d sneaked into the kitchen at night, the number of times I’d hidden my sister’s dolls under the sofa, the number of times I’d promised God I would be good if only He let me wake up tomorrow. I sweated under three quilts while the roses counted and recited, and in the morning the fever broke and the roses were only wallpaper again, but the knowledge stayed: the house was watching, and it had a long memory.

Years later, long after we’d moved away, I drove past the place and saw that someone had painted the clapboards a tasteful charcoal with white trim. The maple we’d planted the year my grandmother died now brushed the second-story windows. Everything looked smaller, of course, but also newly innocent, as though the building had had its record expunged. I parked anyway, walked up the driveway with the casual authority of a ghost, and pressed my palm against the siding. The wood was warm from the sun, but underneath the warmth was a pulse, faint and familiar, the same slow systolic thump I’d felt when I used to hide behind the coats in the front-hall closet, eavesdropping on arguments that always seemed to be about me even when my name was never spoken. The house remembered. I took my hand away and drove off without looking back, but for weeks afterward I dreamed of those roses breaking through drywall like a slow-motion invasion, roots twining around studs, petals unfurling in the dark.

We think we outgrow the structures we inhabit, that we shed them like skins, but really they migrate into us, beam by beam, window by window, until every floorboard we’ve ever trod creaks inside our bones at night. I carry a whole suburb in my knees: the split-level where I learned the difference between a “playdate” and a date date, the colonial where my best friend lived until her father lost the mortgage betting on frozen-orange-juice futures, the raised ranch where I smoked my first joint while the parents were in Naples and the dog kept watch like a repentant gargoyle. Sometimes, walking down an unfamiliar street in a city I’ve never lived in, I’ll catch a whiff of someone’s laundry exhaust—Downy, Gain, the ghost of a teenage bedroom—and the entire cul-de-sac blossoms behind my eyes, complete with sprinkler arcs and the sound of a basketball hitting asphalt at twilight. The neurologists call this involuntary memory; the mystics call it the haptic universe; I call it being perpetually infested by former floor plans.

When I was twenty-four I moved into a studio above a funeral home in a city whose name rhymes with “nowhere.” The rent was cheap because the previous tenant had died in the bathtub, though the landlord was quick to clarify that she’d been alive when the EMTs wheeled her out—technically alive, at any rate. I signed the lease the same afternoon I lost my first adult job, victim of a corporate “restructuring” that felt more like a spell gone wrong. The apartment smelled of lilies and formaldehyde, an olfactory duet that never quite resolved. At night I could hear the embalming machines humming through the pipes, a sound like distant bees. I developed the habit of talking aloud to the woman who’d died, asking her opinion on groceries, on whether I should call my mother back, on how long a person could subsist on Saltines and peanut butter before scurvy set in. She never answered, but the faucet dripped Morse that I refused to translate.

One winter evening the radiator clanged so violently I thought the building was giving birth. Steam hissed, paint peeled in long wet curls, and a rectangle of wallpaper in the “kitchenette” (really a diseased alcove) slid to the floor like a drunk surrendering its license. Behind it was a child’s drawing: a house, a sun wearing sunglasses, a dog with four stick legs and a smile bigger than its head. The crayon lines were still bright, protected from decades of nicotine and sorrow. I stared at that sun and felt suddenly forgiven for every small cowardice I’d ever committed: the thank-you notes I never mailed, the lovers I left without explanation, the cat I didn’t adopt because I feared commitment in eight-pound increments. I patched the wall with grocery-bag paper and left the drawing sealed inside, a seed for the next archeologist.

People say you can’t own a place until you’ve bled in it, but I’ve bled in plenty—paper cuts, nosebleeds, the spectacular shin gash I got while attempting to build an IKEA bookshelf with instructions in Swedish—and none of them ever felt like mine. Ownership, I’ve decided, is not about blood but about breath: the first night you exhale without listening for footsteps, the first time you cook garlic without opening every window, the first time you sing in the shower loud enough to hear yourself over the water. By that metric the only place I’ve ever truly owned was a tent pitched on a glacial moraine in Patagonia, twenty-two kilometers from the nearest road, where the wind tried nightly to roll me into a lake the color of liquid electricity. I sang there—off-key Argentine folk songs, commercial jingles, the national anthem of a country that no longer exists—and no one, not even the mountains, told me to shut up.

Still, we keep trying to build permanence from impermanent things. A friend of mine—architect turned hospice nurse—told me that dying people often confuse the ceiling with the sky. They ask if the clouds will rain, if the stars are out, if the plane overhead is heading home. She strokes their hands and says yes to whatever they need, because what is architecture at the end but a provisional sky, a lid we paint blue and stud with bulbs? I asked her once whether she thought houses felt grief when we left. She laughed, but softly, the way you laugh when someone repeats your own secret thought back to you. “Of course they do,” she said. “They fold in on themselves like dried flowers. They wait for new voices to unwrinkle the air.”

After my grandfather died, my grandmother refused to change a single coaster. She dusted around his eyeglasses, vacuumed under his recliner without moving it an inch. The house became a diorama of grief, every doily a butterfly pinned under glass. When she finally moved to assisted living, the new owners knocked down walls, installed skylights, painted the walnut paneling a cheerful ecru. I toured it once, during an open house that felt like trespassing on daylight. The realtor trilled about “flow” and “natural light,” but all I could hear was the absence of my grandfather’s oxygen machine, the lack of its steady huh-shhh, huh-shhh that used to make the parakeet think spring was coming. I stood in what had been the hallway and felt the house trying to reconcile two timelines, like someone attempting to hum two songs at once. The result was a faint dissonance that prickled the skin, a warning key.

Some nights, when insomnia has me by the throat, I scroll through real-estate listings the way others swipe on dating apps. I ogle crown molding, original hexagon tile, claw-foot tubs that have soaked generations of strangers. I read the descriptors—“sun-drenched,” “charming,” “needs TLC”—as if they were personal ads written by lonely buildings. Sometimes I save a favorite, tell myself that when I win the lottery (which I don’t play) I’ll rescue that Victorian with the widow’s walk and the lilac hedge, drop everything, move in, restore it to dignity. But dawn always arrives, and with it the knowledge that I can barely keep a succulent alive, let alone a staircase with PTSD from a hundred years of soles. Still, the fantasy persists: I will become the steady heartbeat a house requires; it will become the shelter I never quite believe I deserve.

Once, in a city famous for its verticality, I sublet a railroad flat that had once been a bordello. The bedroom still had the original call-box: a brass mouthpiece you could speak into, connected to nothing. I tried it the first night—whispered “hello” into the dark trumpet—and the lights dimmed, though that was probably the fridge cycling on. I slept anyway, dreaming of women in chemises leaning over balustrades, of men tipping bowler hats, of coins rolling across floorboards like metallic rain. In the dream I was both spectator and spectacle: I wore the corset and paid the corset’s price. When I woke, my throat hurt, as if I’d spent the night telling secrets I’d sworn to keep. I left a month early, forfeited the security deposit, but kept the key, which I still find sometimes at the bottom of a jacket pocket, its teeth worn smooth as beach glass.

They say every scar is a doorway, but I think every doorway is also a scar. My body has become a census of thresholds: the hairline fracture in my left wrist from slamming a screen door in a thunderstorm, the half-moon dent in my shin from the trailer hitch of a U-Haul, the burn on my palm from a radiator valve that refused to close. I used to resent these blemishes, but now I collect them the way some people collect passport stamps, proof that I’ve passed through, that I was admitted, that I exited again. The wrist aches when barometric pressure drops, and in that ache I hear the aluminum door bang, see my mother’s face at the kitchen window, feel the storm arrive like a verdict. Pain as souvenir, pain as map.

The pandemic turned us all into reluctant architects. Overnight, bedrooms became offices, kitchen islands became classrooms, closets became meditation studios (or crying chambers, depending on the day). We learned the acoustic properties of every corner: where the Wi-Fi dropped, where the echo made us sound authoritative, where the lighting flattered or condemned. I watched friends flatten their backgrounds into beige nothingness, or else curate bookshelves like museum displays—spines chromatically arranged, the plant positioned just so. We apologized for messes that would once have been invisible. A colleague’s toddler wandered through a meeting naked and suddenly forty strangers knew the color of her potty. Homes, once the last preserve of privacy, became panopticons. I started showering before dawn, as if the virus could travel through hot water, but really because I craved one unrated hour. Sometimes I stood under the spray so long the smoke detector mistook steam for smoke and screamed. Even my alarms were alarmed.

Now the world has mostly reopened, but the partitions linger like phantom limbs. I still position my laptop so the bed is never in frame, still flinch when the doorbell rings even though delivery drivers no longer sprint away like arsonists. The apartment I currently occupy—third floor, walk-up, radiator that sounds like marbles in a blender—has learned my pandemic routines and repeats them back to me. The floor creaks on the exact board I stepped on every day at 2:17 p.m. to check the mail, though I now go days without bothering. The thermostat anticipates my 3 a.m. anxiety spike and pre-chills the room so I won’t sweat through the sheets. Sometimes I catch myself apologizing to the walls for not leaving often enough, the way one apologizes to a dog for a truncated walk.

I wonder what will happen when I finally move out—whether the next tenant will feel the residue of these 876 days of self-quarantine, whether they’ll wake at 3:07 a.m. for reasons they can’t name, whether they’ll dream of mouths没有出现 in tiny Zoom squares. I imagine the landlord repainting, tearing up the carpet, installing a smart fridge that plays podcasts while you chop kale. Still, paint is just makeup; drywall has memory; studs are the ribs of some larger animal which metabolizes our daily exhalations and stores them as atmosphere. One day the building will be demolished, the dust will rise, and each particle will carry a fragment of my 3 a.m. terror, my off-key singing, the smell of the sourdough I nursed like a newborn. The particles will drift, settle, enter other lungs, and those people will dream of a life they never lived, of a staircase that creaks on the seventh step, of yellow roses counting sins.

Sometimes I think the only real home we have is the sentence we’re currently speaking, the breath we’re currently drawing. It’s the one structure we can carry everywhere, the one ceiling that expands rather than collapses when the walls feel like closing in. I’ve started talking to myself again—full conversations, gestures and all—on the premise that I’m practicing for the day when no one else is around to hear the verdict. The other day I caught myself saying, “Remember the wallpaper?” and I realized I wasn’t sure whether I meant the roses in my childhood bedroom or the hidden drawing in the funeral-home studio or the pattern in the bordello call-box flat. Maybe all of them. Maybe memory is just a form of interior decoration, the way we hang our old rooms inside new rooms until the layers grow translucent and every place we’ve ever been becomes a palimpsest, a wallpaper of wallpaper, a breath inside a breath.

And yet, and yet—last week I saw a listing for a cottage on the edge of a lake so small it doesn’t appear on most maps. The photos showed cedar shingles silvered to fish-scale, a wood stove with a mica window glowing like a cat’s eye, a dock sagging under the weight of its own reflection. The description was only three sentences: “Needs love. Needs lungs. Sing here.” I stared at it until the screen timed out and went black, and in the dark rectangle I saw my face superimposed on the cottage, a double exposure. I felt, for the first time in years, the possibility of a door that would open without resistance, a floor that would accept my weight without complaint, a ceiling that would convert my exhale into weather and give it back as birdsong. I closed the tab, but the image persists, hovering like an afterimage when I blink. Maybe one day I’ll contact the agent. Maybe I’ll bring my scant furniture, my succulent in rehab, my box of keys to nowhere. Maybe I’ll learn the names of birds that haven’t yet noticed me. Maybe I’ll sit on that tilting dock and sing every song I know, loud enough for the lake to record, loud enough for the cedar to remember, loud enough for every room I’ve ever abandoned to lean a little closer, listening.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
